text:
Imagine for a second you wake up and it’s mid-January, 2013. Mitt Romney was just inaugurated President of the United States, and the damage from the worst economic crisis since the Great Depression heals slowly and steadily. There is no Dem president for Americans to be tired of in 2016, the populism that swept the globe stays contained to Europe, but Hillary Clinton still loses the election, meaning Romney gets reelected. Trump tried to topple him in the primaries but failed, and he will never try to run for prez again. In 2020 the contest is hot, since there won’t be an incumbent. Ron DeSantis makes it to the general and faces off with Pete Buttigieg. Pete wins in a landslide, energizing the youth and giving the US its first woman VP in Kamala Harris, but during his time in office, his popularity is in constant decline due to his bureaucratic ways of running the executive branch and his overregulation.

It’s now September 17th, 2024. Two debates have taken place between Pete and DeSantis, and one more is scheduled. The contest is red hot, engaging, and entertaining. The candidates are young. Pete keeps his VP pick while DeSantis is campaigning alongside Vivek Ramaswamy. It’s a hype cycle on the scale of 2016, with less vitriol. It’s fun. You’ll tell your kids about the whale of a time this cycle was, how you and your fellas got plastered watching the debates, how you’d be at the poker table making jokes about each candidate, and how almost everyone you knew voted. You’ll use these stories to convey why your kids should vote too.

Except if you’re reading this you’re already awake. This sucks.

text:
Obama: "When I took office, gasoline was $1.88."
Wrong! He lies!! It was $3.27 in his election year. 
Records on gas prices began in 1929. 
1929] $0.21
By decade, Lowest and Highest (year).
1930s] L: $0.17 (31); H: $0.20 (37, 38)
1940s] L: $0.18 (40); H: $0.27 (49)
1950s] L: $0.27 (50-52); H: $0.31 (57, 59)
1960s] L: $0.30 (63, 64); H: $0.35 (69)
1970s] L: $0.36 (70-72); H: $0.86 (79) (Crisis begins in 72)
1980s] L: $0.86 (86); H: $1.31 (81)
1990s] L: $1.06 (98); H: $1.23 (96, 97)
2000s] L: $1.36 (02); H: $3.27 (08) 
2010s] L: $2.14 (16); H: $3.80 (12)

These are National Averages. Your local gas costs may be or may have been significantly different, like here in Los Angeles, CA, reached over $5.00 at one point, likely in 2008.

Sources: TitleMax Discovery.
